feed.title: Wasil Zafar — Physical sciences
feed.subtitle: Latest physical sciences articles from Wasil Zafar

entry.title: Part 1: Prerequisites & Foundations — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: Before we can explore how the universe behaves at extreme speeds and massive scales, we need a solid mathematical toolkit. Think of these mathematical tools as the language in which nature expresses its deepest secrets. Without this language, Einstein's elegant equations would be incomprehensible sy

entry.title: Part 2: Crisis of Classical Physics — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: By the late 19th century, physicists were confident they had nearly completed the picture of the universe. Newton's mechanics explained motion from falling apples to orbiting planets. Maxwell's electromagnetism unified electricity, magnetism, and light. Thermodynamics governed heat and energy. The u

entry.title: Part 3: Special Relativity — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: In June 1905, Albert Einstein published "On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies" — a paper that would redefine our understanding of space and time. Its foundation rests on just two postulates, both seemingly innocuous, yet together producing revolutionary consequences. This means there's no "absolu

entry.title: Part 4: Spacetime & Geometry — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: In 1908, Hermann Minkowski — Einstein's former mathematics professor — delivered a famous lecture that began: "Henceforth space by itself, and time by itself, are doomed to fade away into mere shadows, and only a kind of union of the two will preserve an independent reality." This was not poetry — i

entry.title: Part 5: General Relativity — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: For over 200 years, Newton's law of gravitation reigned supreme. It described gravity as a force acting instantaneously across space — a mysterious "action at a distance" that Newton himself found philosophically troubling. He famously wrote: "I have not been able to discover the cause of those prop

entry.title: Part 6: Cosmology — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: In the early 20th century, most scientists — including Einstein himself — believed the universe was static and eternal. It had always existed and would always exist, unchanging in its large-scale structure. This assumption was so deeply ingrained that when Einstein's own equations of General Relativ

entry.title: Part 7: Experimental Verification — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: Einstein himself proposed three classical tests when he published General Relativity in 1915. These tests — the perihelion precession of Mercury, the deflection of starlight by the Sun, and gravitational redshift — formed the initial pillars of experimental verification. Each addressed a prediction

entry.title: Part 8: Mathematical Relativity — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: General Relativity requires a mathematical language that works in any coordinate system — one where the laws of physics look the same regardless of whether we use Cartesian, spherical, or any arbitrary coordinates. This language is tensor calculus . A tensor is a geometric object that transforms in

entry.title: Part 9: Advanced Topics & Frontiers — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: General Relativity and Quantum Mechanics are the two pillars of modern physics — yet they are fundamentally incompatible. GR treats spacetime as a smooth, continuous manifold curved by matter-energy. Quantum mechanics describes nature in terms of discrete quanta, superposition, and uncertainty. When

entry.title: Part 10: Applications of Relativity — Theory of Relativity
entry.summary: Einstein published his theory of Special Relativity in 1905 and General Relativity in 1915. For decades, these were considered elegant but impractical — beautiful mathematics describing extreme conditions far removed from daily life. Today, relativistic corrections are essential for technologies tha
